Methods for Conducting Electrical Motor Efficiency Audits:
paragraphs: Increasing energy efficiency is the cleanest, most cost-effective, and fastest way to provide reliable electricity. Motors represent around 46% of all electricity consumed and 69% of total energy used in the industrial sector . Energy consumption is one of the largest challenges facing many organi z ations. The inadequate architecture and energy consumption must be addressed first. Poor design and misuse are the primary reasons for high energy costs and the inability to meet the increasing demand for electricity. Many motors operate between 50 and 100 percent of rated load, however the most efficient operating occurs at around 75% of rated load, and efficiency tends to decrease with age. At 50% load, an electric motor’s efficiency begins to decrease automatically.

75% of industrial motor failures result in between one and six days of yearly plant downtime. 90% of motor failures occurred within one month of the initial warning, and 36% occurred on the same day. To prevent motor failure and operational concerns, it is necessary to: Regular evaluations, analyses, inspections, and audits can aid in the reduction of electrical motor difficulties and improvement of motor performance.

During a 53-year period, improve the performance of an induction motor by 68%. (1960 to 2013). To raise the annual efficiency of an electrical induction motor, it is necessary to reduce losses. Over time, the energy efficiency of induction motors has grown (data of 45 kW, 4p design motor). Carelabs evaluates the performance and durability of electrical motors in a variety of industrial and commercial settings. We investigate and diagnose faults so that you may reduce losses and increase motor performance. As skilled and seasoned professionals, it is our objective to provide superior services to our clients.

Carelabs provides motor acceleration analysis to ensure that the motor performs correctly under extreme loads and to increase productivity (by mitigating the losses). Improving motor reliability by decreasing losses. We provide motor start analysis services in every major Swiss city, including Zurich, Geneva, Basel, and Bern.
